title: Quiz: FPGA-Based Quantum Control
description: Test your understanding of FPGA architectures, pulse sequencing, and real-time control for quantum computing systems

questions.question: Why are FPGAs preferred over CPUs for quantum control applications?

options.text: FPGAs are cheaper and easier to program
FPGAs provide deterministic timing and lower latency through hardware parallelism
FPGAs consume less power than CPUs
FPGAs have more memory than CPUs

questions.explanation: FPGAs are preferred because they provide deterministic timing (guaranteed execution times) and very low latency (10-100 ns vs 10-100 microseconds for CPUs). This hardware-level parallelism is essential for real-time feedback within qubit coherence times. While power consumption can be lower for specific applications, the primary advantage is timing determinism.

questions.question: A 6-input Look-Up Table (LUT) in an FPGA can implement:

options.text: Only specific predefined logic functions
Any Boolean function of 6 input variables
Only combinational logic, not sequential
A maximum of 6 different outputs

questions.explanation: A 6-input LUT can implement any Boolean function of 6 variables by storing all 2^6 = 64 possible output values. This is a fundamental property of LUT-based FPGA architecture - complete generality for functions of the specified number of inputs.

questions.question: In a typical FPGA-based quantum control system, what is the approximate total feedback latency from measurement to conditional pulse output?

options.text: 1-10 nanoseconds
200-500 nanoseconds
10-50 microseconds
1-10 milliseconds

questions.explanation: State-of-the-art FPGA-based quantum control systems achieve total feedback latencies of 200-500 ns. This includes ADC sampling, digital demodulation, integration, threshold decision, and DAC output. This is fast enough for real-time feedback within typical qubit coherence times of 50-200 microseconds.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of DSP slices in FPGAs used for quantum control?

options.text: Long-term data storage
Clock generation and distribution
Efficient multiply-accumulate operations for demodulation and filtering
Communication with external devices

questions.explanation: DSP slices are hardened multiply-accumulate (MAC) units optimized for digital signal processing operations. In quantum control, they are essential for efficient IQ demodulation, digital filtering, and waveform calculations - all of which require many multiply-accumulate operations.

questions.question: In FPGA-based quantum control, waveform memory is unlimited because FPGAs can dynamically allocate memory as needed.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. FPGA Block RAM (BRAM) is limited and fixed at design time. Waveform memory is a precious resource that must be carefully managed. Memory optimization strategies like parameterized waveforms, compression, and on-the-fly generation are often necessary to fit all required waveforms.

questions.question: For digital IQ demodulation in an FPGA, the signal is multiplied by:

options.text: A constant DC value
Cosine (I) and sine (Q) reference signals at the intermediate frequency
The original input signal itself
A random noise signal for dithering

questions.explanation: Digital IQ demodulation extracts the in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) components by multiplying the sampled signal by cosine and sine references at the intermediate frequency, then integrating. This extracts the amplitude and phase information needed to determine the qubit state.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid techniques for optimizing FPGA critical path latency? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Pipelining - breaking long combinational paths with registers
Retiming - redistributing registers for balanced pipeline stages
Resource duplication - parallel processing paths
Increasing clock frequency without any circuit changes

questions.explanation: Pipelining, retiming, and resource duplication are all valid optimization techniques. Pipelining adds registers to break long paths, retiming moves existing registers for better balance, and duplication creates parallel paths. Simply increasing clock frequency without circuit changes will violate timing constraints.

questions.question: What is 'timing closure' in FPGA design?

options.text: Shutting down the FPGA at specific times to save power
Ensuring all signal paths meet setup and hold time requirements at the target clock frequency
Synchronizing the FPGA clock with external instruments
Closing unused I/O pins to prevent noise

questions.explanation: Timing closure means that all paths in the design meet the setup and hold time requirements at the target clock frequency. This ensures reliable operation - signals arrive at flip-flops with enough margin before and after the clock edge. Failing to achieve timing closure results in unreliable, non-deterministic behavior.
